nodes.label: Adams-Fournier, Sobolev Spaces, Theorem 6.3 (Rellich-Kondrachov)
nodes.statement: Classical Rellich-Kondrachov compact embedding theorem: if $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is bounded with Lipschitz boundary and $kp < n$, then $W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow\hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega)$ for $1 \leq q < p^* = np/(n - kp)$.

metadata.source: R. A. Adams and J. J. F. Fournier, Sobolev Spaces, 2nd ed., Academic Press, 2003, Theorem 6.3

nodes.label: Muckenhoupt, A_p weight theory
nodes.statement: Theory of Muckenhoupt $A_p$ weights: $w \in A_p$ if and only if the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator $M$ is bounded on $L^p(w)$, and the $A_p$ condition reads $\sup_Q \left(\frac{1}{|Q|}\int_Q w\right)\left(\frac{1}{|Q|}\int_Q w^{-1/(p-1)}\right)^{p-1} < \infty$.

metadata.source: B. Muckenhoupt, Weighted norm inequalities for the Hardy maximal function, Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. 165 (1972), 207--226

nodes.label: Turesson, Nonlinear Potential Theory and Weighted Sobolev Spaces, Proposition 3.6
nodes.statement: For $w \in A_p$ and $\Omega$ bounded Lipschitz, the space $C^\infty(\overline{\Omega})$ is dense in $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$.

metadata.source: B. O. Turesson, Nonlinear Potential Theory and Weighted Sobolev Spaces, Lecture Notes in Math. 1736, Springer, 2000, Proposition 3.6

nodes.label: Assumption 1.1 (Domain regularity)
nodes.statement: Throughout this paper, $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ denotes a bounded domain with Lipschitz boundary $\partial\Omega$, and $n \geq 2$.

nodes.label: Assumption 1.2 (Weight class)
nodes.statement: The weight function $w: \Omega \to (0,\infty)$ belongs to the Muckenhoupt class $A_p$ for some $1 < p < \infty$, with $[w]_{A_p} \leq K$ for a fixed constant $K > 0$.

nodes.label: Notation 1.3 (Weighted Lebesgue space)
nodes.statement: For $1 \leq p < \infty$ and a weight $w$, we write $L^p(\Omega, w)$ for the space of measurable functions $u$ with $\|u\|_{L^p(w)} := \left(\int_\Omega |u(x)|^p w(x)\,dx\right)^{1/p} < \infty$.

nodes.label: Definition 2.1 (Weighted Sobolev space)
nodes.statement: For $k \in \mathbb{N}_0$, $1 < p < \infty$, and $w \in A_p$, the weighted Sobolev space $W^{k,p}(\Omega, w)$ is the completion of $C^\infty(\overline{\Omega})$ under the norm $\|u\|_{W^{k,p}(w)} := \left(\sum_{|\alpha| \leq k} \int_\Omega |D^\alpha u(x)|^p w(x)\,dx\right)^{1/p}$.

nodes.label: Definition 2.2 (Weighted Sobolev space with zero boundary values)
nodes.statement: The space $W_0^{k,p}(\Omega, w)$ is defined as the closure of $C_c^\infty(\Omega)$ in $W^{k,p}(\Omega, w)$.

nodes.label: Definition 2.3 ($A_p$ characteristic)
nodes.statement: For $w \in A_p$ with $1 < p < \infty$, the $A_p$ characteristic of $w$ is $[w]_{A_p} := \sup_Q \left(\frac{1}{|Q|}\int_Q w\right)\left(\frac{1}{|Q|}\int_Q w^{-p'/p}\right)^{p/p'}$ where the supremum is over all cubes $Q \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ and $p' = p/(p-1)$.

nodes.label: Notation 2.4 (Embedding arrows)
nodes.statement: We write $X \hookrightarrow Y$ for a continuous embedding and $X \hookrightarrow\hookrightarrow Y$ for a compact embedding of Banach spaces.

nodes.label: Lemma 2.5 (Self-improvement of $A_p$ weights)
nodes.statement: If $w \in A_p$ with $1 < p < \infty$, then there exists $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(n, p, [w]_{A_p}) > 0$ such that $w \in A_{p - \varepsilon}$. In particular, $w \in A_q$ for some $q < p$.

nodes.label: Remark 2.6 (Doubling property)
nodes.statement: Every $A_p$ weight $w$ induces a doubling measure $w\,dx$: there exists $C_d > 0$ such that $w(2Q) \leq C_d\, w(Q)$ for every cube $Q$. This makes $(\Omega, w\,dx)$ a space of homogeneous type.

nodes.label: Proposition 2.7 (Weighted Poincare inequality)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega$ satisfy Assumption 1.1 and $w \in A_p$. Then there exists $C = C(n, p, [w]_{A_p}, \Omega) > 0$ such that for all $u \in W_0^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$, $\|u\|_{L^p(w)} \leq C\,\mathrm{diam}(\Omega)\,\|\nabla u\|_{L^p(w)}$.

nodes.label: Lemma 3.1 (Cutoff function estimate)
nodes.statement: For any $\delta > 0$, there exists a family of cutoff functions $\{\varphi_\delta\} \subset C_c^\infty(\Omega)$ with $0 \leq \varphi_\delta \leq 1$, $\varphi_\delta = 1$ on $\Omega_\delta := \{x \in \Omega : \mathrm{dist}(x, \partial\Omega) > \delta\}$, and $\|\nabla \varphi_\delta\|_{L^\infty} \leq C/\delta$ for a constant $C$ depending only on $\Omega$.

nodes.label: Lemma 3.2 (Local compactness)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega' \Subset \Omega$ and $w \in A_p$. Then the restriction map $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w) \to L^p(\Omega', w)$ is compact.

nodes.label: Lemma 3.3 (Boundary tail estimate)
nodes.statement: Let $w \in A_p$ and let $\Omega_\delta = \{x \in \Omega : \mathrm{dist}(x, \partial\Omega) > \delta\}$. For every bounded sequence $(u_j)$ in $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$, $\lim_{\delta \to 0} \sup_j \int_{\Omega \setminus \Omega_\delta} |u_j|^p w\,dx = 0$.

nodes.label: Definition 3.4 (Weight exponent gap)
nodes.statement: Given $w \in A_p$, define the weight exponent gap $\gamma(w, p) := p - \inf\{q > 1 : w \in A_q\}$. By the self-improvement property, $\gamma(w, p) > 0$ whenever $w \in A_p$ with $p > 1$.

nodes.label: Remark 3.5 (Role of the exponent gap)
nodes.statement: The quantity $\gamma(w, p)$ controls how much room is available for the Sobolev embedding to absorb the weight. When $\gamma$ is large, the weight is far from the critical $A_p$ threshold and compactness arguments have more margin.

nodes.label: Theorem 4.1 (Compact embedding into weighted $L^p$)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a bounded Lipschitz domain, $1 < p < \infty$, and $w \in A_p$. Then $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w) \hookrightarrow\hookrightarrow L^p(\Omega, w)$.

nodes.label: Theorem 4.3 (Compact embedding into weighted $L^q$ for subcritical $q$)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a bounded Lipschitz domain, $1 < p < n$, and $w \in A_p$. Then for every $1 \leq q < p_w^* := np/(n - p + \gamma(w,p))$, the embedding $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w) \hookrightarrow\hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega, w)$ is compact.

nodes.label: Lemma 4.2 (Uniform integrability)
nodes.statement: Let $(u_j)$ be a bounded sequence in $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$ with $w \in A_p$. Then $(|u_j|^p w)$ is uniformly integrable on $\Omega$.

nodes.label: Corollary 4.4 (Compact embedding for $W_0^{1,p}$)
nodes.statement: Under the hypotheses of Theorem 4.1, $W_0^{1,p}(\Omega, w) \hookrightarrow\hookrightarrow L^p(\Omega, w)$. Moreover, the Poincare inequality (Proposition 2.7) shows that $\|\nabla u\|_{L^p(w)}$ is an equivalent norm on $W_0^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$.

nodes.label: Remark 4.5 (Sharpness of the critical exponent)
nodes.statement: The critical exponent $p_w^* = np/(n - p + \gamma(w,p))$ in Theorem 4.3 is sharp in the following sense: when $w(x) = |x|^\alpha$ with $\alpha = (p-1-\varepsilon)$ for small $\varepsilon > 0$, one can construct a sequence in $W^{1,p}(B_1, w)$ that is bounded but has no convergent subsequence in $L^{p_w^*}(B_1, w)$.

nodes.label: Proposition 5.1 (Spectral gap for weighted Laplacian)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega$ and $w$ be as in Assumption 1.1--1.2, and let $-\Delta_w$ denote the weighted Laplacian on $W_0^{1,2}(\Omega, w)$. Then $-\Delta_w$ has compact resolvent and its spectrum consists of a sequence of eigenvalues $0 < \lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2 \leq \cdots \to \infty$.

nodes.label: Corollary 5.2 (Weighted Fredholm alternative)
nodes.statement: For $f \in L^2(\Omega, w)$ and $\lambda \notin \{\lambda_k\}_{k=1}^\infty$, the problem $-\Delta_w u - \lambda u = f$ in $\Omega$, $u = 0$ on $\partial\Omega$, has a unique weak solution $u \in W_0^{1,2}(\Omega, w)$.

nodes.label: Example 5.3 (Power weight on the unit ball)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega = B_1(0) \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ and $w(x) = |x|^\alpha$ with $-n < \alpha < n(p-1)$. Then $w \in A_p$ and Theorem 4.1 yields $W^{1,p}(B_1, |x|^\alpha) \hookrightarrow\hookrightarrow L^p(B_1, |x|^\alpha)$. The weight exponent gap satisfies $\gamma(w,p) = p - 1 - \alpha/n$.

nodes.label: Example 5.4 (Distance-to-boundary weight)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega$ be a bounded Lipschitz domain and $w(x) = \mathrm{dist}(x, \partial\Omega)^\beta$ with $-1 < \beta < p - 1$. Then $w \in A_p$ and Theorem 4.3 applies with $\gamma(w,p) = p - 1 - \beta$.

nodes.label: Theorem 5.5 (Existence for weighted semilinear problem)
nodes.statement: Let $\Omega$, $w$ be as in Assumptions 1.1--1.2, $p = 2$, and let $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous with $|g(t)| \leq C(1 + |t|^{q-1})$ for some $1 < q < 2_w^*$. Then the problem $-\Delta_w u = g(u)$ in $\Omega$, $u = 0$ on $\partial\Omega$, admits at least one weak solution in $W_0^{1,2}(\Omega, w)$.

nodes.label: Conjecture 5.6 (Optimal Holder regularity)
nodes.statement: Under the hypotheses of Theorem 5.5, the weak solution $u$ belongs to $C^{0,\alpha}(\overline{\Omega})$ for some $\alpha = \alpha(n, p, [w]_{A_p}) > 0$.

nodes.label: Lemma A.1 (Reverse Holder inequality for $A_p$ weights)
nodes.statement: If $w \in A_p$ with $1 < p < \infty$, then there exists $r = r(n, p, [w]_{A_p}) > 1$ such that $\left(\frac{1}{|Q|}\int_Q w^r\right)^{1/r} \leq \frac{C}{|Q|}\int_Q w$ for all cubes $Q$, with $C = C(n, p, [w]_{A_p})$.

metadata.source: Proof adapted from Grafakos, Classical Fourier Analysis, Theorem 9.2.2

nodes.label: Lemma A.2 (Weighted maximal function bound)
nodes.statement: For $w \in A_p$ with $1 < p < \infty$, the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator satisfies $\|Mf\|_{L^p(w)} \leq C_{n,p}\,[w]_{A_p}^{1/(p-1)}\,\|f\|_{L^p(w)}$ for all $f \in L^p(\Omega, w)$.

edges.detail: The norm of $W^{k,p}(\Omega, w)$ is built from the $L^p(w)$ norms of derivatives, relying on the notation and definition of weighted Lebesgue spaces
Definition 2.1 requires $w \in A_p$, referring explicitly to Assumption 1.2
$W_0^{k,p}(\Omega, w)$ is defined as a closed subspace of $W^{k,p}(\Omega, w)$
Definition 2.3 recalls the classical Muckenhoupt $A_p$ condition from [Muc72]
The self-improvement lemma is stated in terms of the $A_p$ characteristic $[w]_{A_p}$
Self-improvement is a consequence of the reverse Holder inequality, part of the classical $A_p$ theory
The doubling property follows from the $A_p$ condition; the remark references the $A_p$ characteristic implicitly
The Poincare inequality is stated for functions in $W_0^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$, citing Definition 2.2
Proof begins by invoking the Lipschitz boundary condition from Assumption 1.1
The proof applies Lemma 2.5 to find $q < p$ with $w \in A_q$, enabling the use of a chaining argument on cubes

metadata.proof_step: step 2: self-improvement to pass to a smaller exponent

edges.detail: Density of $C^\infty(\overline{\Omega})$ in $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$ is cited from Turesson [Tur00, Prop. 3.6]
The Lipschitz regularity of $\partial\Omega$ is needed to construct smooth cutoff functions with controlled gradient
The proof reduces to the classical (unweighted) Rellich-Kondrachov theorem on the interior subdomain via weight comparability
The bounded sequence lives in $W^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$ as defined in Definition 2.1
The doubling property is used to show that $w$ is comparable to a constant on compact subsets of $\Omega$
Cutoff functions from Lemma 3.1 are used to isolate the boundary layer

metadata.proof_step: step 1: localize near the boundary using cutoff functions

edges.detail: The weighted Poincare inequality is applied on thin boundary strips to bound $\int_{\Omega \setminus \Omega_\delta} |u|^p w\,dx$ by the gradient term
The $A_p$ condition on $w$ is essential for the boundary-layer decay estimate
The reverse Holder inequality (Lemma A.1) provides the higher integrability of $w$ needed in step 3 of the proof

metadata.proof_step: step 3: higher integrability to close the iteration

edges.detail: The positivity $\gamma(w,p) > 0$ follows immediately from the self-improvement lemma
The remark interprets the quantity $\gamma(w,p)$ defined in Definition 3.4
Step 1 of the proof extracts a subsequence convergent in $L^p(\Omega', w)$ for every $\Omega' \Subset \Omega$ via Lemma 3.2
Step 2 uses the boundary tail estimate (Lemma 3.3) to show that the $L^p(w)$-mass near $\partial\Omega$ is uniformly small
Step 3 invokes uniform integrability (Lemma 4.2) and Vitali's convergence theorem to upgrade a.e. convergence to $L^p(w)$ convergence
The bounded Lipschitz domain hypothesis is invoked at the start of the proof
The $A_p$ condition on $w$ is a standing hypothesis
The domain and range spaces of the embedding rely on Definition 2.1
Theorem 4.1 extends the classical Rellich-Kondrachov theorem (Adams-Fournier, Thm 6.3) from unweighted to $A_p$-weighted Sobolev spaces
Self-improvement yields $w \in A_{p-\varepsilon}$, which provides the extra integrability needed for the de la Vallee-Poussin criterion

metadata.proof_step: step 1: extract higher integrability via self-improvement

edges.detail: The reverse Holder inequality from Lemma A.1 is applied to bound $\int |u_j|^{p+\varepsilon} w\,dx$ uniformly
The $q = p$ case is handled by Theorem 4.1; the proof then interpolates for $q < p$ and bootstraps for $p < q < p_w^*$
The critical exponent $p_w^*$ is defined in terms of $\gamma(w,p)$ from Definition 3.4
Self-improvement is used to shift the integrability exponent, gaining room below the critical threshold
The weighted maximal function bound (Lemma A.2) is used to control a truncated Riesz potential in the bootstrapping step
Boundary tail control from Lemma 3.3 is reused for the higher-exponent compactness argument
Bounded Lipschitz domain assumption is a standing hypothesis
$A_p$ weight assumption is a standing hypothesis
Theorem 4.3 generalizes the classical Rellich-Kondrachov theorem: setting $w \equiv 1$ recovers $\gamma = p-1$ and $p_w^* = p^*$
Corollary 4.4 specializes Theorem 4.1 to the closed subspace $W_0^{1,p}(\Omega, w)$
The Poincare inequality from Proposition 2.7 is used to show norm equivalence on $W_0^{1,p}$
The remark demonstrates sharpness of the critical exponent in Theorem 4.3 via an explicit counterexample
The counterexample computes $\gamma(w,p)$ explicitly for the power weight $|x|^\alpha$
Compact embedding of $W_0^{1,2}(\Omega, w)$ into $L^2(\Omega, w)$ is the key input for the spectral theorem
The weighted Poincare inequality ensures coercivity of the bilinear form associated to $-\Delta_w$
The Fredholm alternative is a direct consequence of the compact resolvent established in Proposition 5.1
Example 5.3 applies Theorem 4.1 to the specific weight $w(x) = |x|^\alpha$ on $B_1(0)$
The weight exponent gap $\gamma$ is computed explicitly for the power weight
Example 5.4 applies Theorem 4.3 to the distance-to-boundary weight
The exponent gap $\gamma$ is computed for the distance weight $\mathrm{dist}(x, \partial\Omega)^\beta$
The compact embedding $W_0^{1,2}(\Omega, w) \hookrightarrow\hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega, w)$ for $q < 2_w^*$ is used to verify the Palais-Smale condition

metadata.proof_step: step 2: Palais-Smale condition via compact embedding

edges.detail: The coercivity and compact embedding of $W_0^{1,2}(\Omega, w)$ underlie the variational framework
The Poincare inequality ensures that the energy functional is coercive on $W_0^{1,2}(\Omega, w)$
Domain regularity is a standing hypothesis
$A_p$ weight assumption is a standing hypothesis
The conjecture concerns the regularity of solutions produced by Theorem 5.5
The proof is formulated entirely in terms of the $A_p$ characteristic from Definition 2.3
The proof follows the classical Coifman-Fefferman approach to the reverse Holder inequality
The sharp constant $[w]_{A_p}^{1/(p-1)}$ involves the $A_p$ characteristic
The proof of the sharp weighted bound for $M$ uses the reverse Holder inequality from Lemma A.1
The sharp constant is attributed to Buckley (1993), building on the original Muckenhoupt theory
The weighted Poincare inequality is used implicitly in the passage from gradient control to function control in the interior compactness argument
The $A_p$ condition ensures weight comparability on compact subsets
